title: Topic 151: Toxicity in Online Games: Why People Are Meaner Behind Screens
content:
## Topic 151: Toxicity in Online Games: Why People Are Meaner Behind Screens

### ABSTRACT

This paper delves into the fascinating, and often infuriating, phenomenon of online toxicity, specifically within the realm of video games. We explore the psychological underpinnings of why your average mild-mannered accountant transforms into a trash-talking, keyboard-smashing beast when given an anonymous online avatar. Through a rigorous, and frankly hilarious, analysis of existing literature, case studies, and real-world examples, we dissect the roles of deindividuation, the online disinhibition effect, and the Greater Internet F***wad Theory. This paper not only sheds light on the darker side of human nature when the social leash is loosened but also provides a surprisingly insightful commentary on consumer behavior and the gaming industry's complicity in creating these digital Colosseums. Prepare for a journey into the heart of digital darkness, where the only thing more savage than the gameplay is the chat log.

### INTRODUCTION

Picture the scene: you, a valiant hero, are navigating the treacherous landscapes of a popular online multiplayer game. You’ve spent hours honing your skills, your reflexes are sharp, and your strategy is flawless. You’re on the cusp of a glorious victory when, suddenly, a teammate makes a minor, almost imperceptible, error. The chat window erupts. A torrent of abuse, insults, and creative profanity floods the screen, directed at the unfortunate soul who dared to be human. Welcome to the world of online gaming, where the anonymity of a screen name can turn even the most placid individual into a raging digital barbarian.

This phenomenon, affectionately known as “toxicity,” is as integral to the online gaming experience as lag and overpriced cosmetic items. But what is it about the digital realm that brings out the absolute worst in us? Why do people say things to strangers online that they would never dream of uttering in a face-to-face conversation? Is it the anonymity? The lack of immediate consequences? Or is there something deeper, something about the very nature of online interaction that strips away our social graces and leaves us with our raw, unfiltered ids?

This paper will embark on a scholarly, yet unapologetically humorous, investigation into the psychology of online toxicity. We will explore the key theories that attempt to explain this behavior, from the well-established concept of deindividuation to the more colloquially named, but no less accurate, “Greater Internet F***wad Theory.” We will examine how game design itself can foster a toxic environment, and we will look at the real-world consequences of this digital vitriol. So, grab your popcorn, mute your microphone, and prepare to dive headfirst into the glorious, chaotic, and utterly fascinating world of online toxicity. You might just learn something about yourself, and you’ll definitely learn some new and creative insults.

### LITERATURE REVIEW

The academic world, in its infinite wisdom, has not been idle in the face of this digital deluge of dickishness. Scholars, likely after being on the receiving end of a particularly nasty “gg ez” after a crushing defeat, have been studying the phenomenon of online aggression for decades. A central pillar in understanding this behavior is the concept of **deindividuation**, a term that sounds like it was invented by a sociologist who just learned how to use a thesaurus. First proposed by Festinger, Pepitone, and Newcomb in 1952, deindividuation refers to a state in which an individual’s sense of self-awareness and personal responsibility is diminished within a group setting [1]. In the context of online gaming, the “group” is the vast, anonymous sea of players, and the individual becomes a faceless avatar, a mere cog in the machine of digital carnage. This anonymity, as many a troll has gleefully discovered, is a powerful elixir. As one article from *The Psychology of Games* astutely points out, the anonymity provided by the internet can lead to what is known as the “online disinhibition effect,” where the lack of social cues and accountability encourages people to behave in ways they wouldn’t in the real world [2].

Further research has built upon this foundation, exploring the nuances of online toxicity. A study by Tang and Fox (2016) found that certain personality traits, such as low agreeableness and high neuroticism, were predictors of online harassment, suggesting that some people are just naturally predisposed to being jerks online [3]. But it’s not just about individual personality. The environment itself plays a crucial role. Research by Kou and Nardi (2014) on *League of Legends* players identified specific in-game triggers for toxic behavior, such as perceived unfairness and poor team performance [4]. This suggests that the very design of competitive games, with their emphasis on winning and individual performance metrics, can create a breeding ground for frustration and, consequently, toxicity. As Constance Steinkuehler of UC Irvine notes, the lack of “guardrails” in many online games essentially “platforms cruelty” [5]. The industry’s reluctance to implement robust moderation systems, often citing freedom of speech or the potential impact on their bottom line, has allowed toxicity to fester and become normalized. Indeed, a 2021 study by Beres et al. found that the normalization of toxicity in online gaming communities is a significant problem, with many players coming to see it as an unavoidable, and even acceptable, part of the culture [6]. It seems that in the digital arena, we have collectively agreed that it’s perfectly fine to be awful to each other, as long as it’s in the name of “fun.”

### ANALYSIS

So, what have we learned from our esteemed colleagues in academia? We’ve learned that the internet, and online games in particular, can be a cesspool of human misery. But *why*? Let’s break it down with the scientific rigor of a high school biology dissection, but with more jokes and less formaldehyde.

The primary culprit, as we’ve seen, is **anonymity**. When you’re just a username and an avatar, the social contract that governs our everyday interactions gets thrown out the window. You’re not John Smith, the friendly neighbor who waters his petunias every morning; you’re “xX_DragonSlayer_420_Xx,” and you’re here to chew bubblegum and scream at your teammates… and you’re all out of bubblegum. This sense of detachment from one’s real-world identity is the cornerstone of deindividuation. It’s the same psychological principle that leads to mob mentality, where individuals in a large crowd feel less accountable for their actions. In the digital world, the crowd is global, and the accountability is virtually nonexistent.

But anonymity is just one piece of the puzzle. The **lack of non-verbal cues** is another major factor. In a face-to-face conversation, we rely on a multitude of signals to gauge the other person’s emotional state: facial expressions, body language, tone of voice. Online, all of that is stripped away. A sarcastic comment can be easily misinterpreted as a genuine insult. A friendly jab can feel like a personal attack. Without the rich tapestry of non-verbal communication, we’re left with the cold, hard text on the screen, and our brains, being the paranoid, overthinking machines that they are, often assume the worst. This is why a simple “?” in the chat after you make a mistake can feel like a dagger to the heart.

Furthermore, the very **design of many online games** can be a catalyst for toxicity. Competitive games, by their nature, are high-stakes environments. When your rank, your reputation, and your digital ego are on the line, tensions are bound to run high. Add to that the frustration of dealing with teammates who may not be as skilled or as dedicated as you are, and you have a recipe for disaster. Game developers, in their quest to create engaging and challenging experiences, have inadvertently created perfect petri dishes for growing toxic cultures. The constant feedback loops of wins and losses, the public display of performance statistics, and the emphasis on individual skill can all contribute to a hyper-competitive, and often hostile, environment. It’s no wonder that games like *League of Legends* and *Overwatch*, with their intense team-based competition, are notorious for their toxic communities.

Finally, we have the **normalization of toxicity**. As Constance Steinkuehler’s research shows, many players, especially younger ones, have become desensitized to toxic behavior [5]. They see it as a normal, even expected, part of the gaming experience. This creates a vicious cycle: the more toxicity people are exposed to, the more likely they are to become toxic themselves, either as a defense mechanism or simply because they don’t know any better. The result is a self-perpetuating culture of negativity, where being a decent human being is seen as a sign of weakness. It’s a digital Lord of the Flies, but with more memes and less pig-sticking.

### REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES

Lest you think we’re just blowing hot air, let’s look at some concrete examples of online toxicity in action. These are the stories from the digital trenches, the cautionary tales that remind us that behind every avatar is a real person, and sometimes, that person is a real jerk.

**The *League of Legends* Experience:** No discussion of online toxicity would be complete without mentioning *League of Legends*, the undisputed king of toxic communities. For years, this game has been the poster child for everything that is wrong with online gaming culture. The competitive nature of the game, combined with a notoriously ineffective reporting system, has created a breeding ground for some of the most creative and vile insults ever conceived by humankind. One need only browse the *League of Legends* subreddit for a few minutes to find a treasure trove of horror stories: players intentionally feeding the enemy team, holding the game hostage by refusing to play, and, of course, the endless stream of verbal abuse. Riot Games, the developer of *League of Legends*, has made numerous attempts to combat this toxicity, from implementing an honor system to using machine learning to detect and punish toxic players. Yet, the problem persists, a testament to the sheer, unadulterated rage that a 5v5 fantasy battle can induce.

**The Rise and Fall of *Overwatch*:** When *Overwatch* was first released, it was hailed as a breath of fresh air. Its colorful characters, emphasis on teamwork, and positive, upbeat tone seemed to be the antidote to the grimdark toxicity of other competitive games. But, as with all things on the internet, the honeymoon period was short-lived. As the game’s competitive scene grew, so too did the toxicity. Players became obsessed with team composition, hero picks, and individual performance, and anyone who deviated from the established “meta” was met with a barrage of criticism. The game’s voice chat, once a tool for coordination and camaraderie, became a vector for abuse. Blizzard, the developer of *Overwatch*, has struggled to keep up with the rising tide of toxicity, implementing endorsement systems and new reporting tools with mixed results. The story of *Overwatch* is a tragic one, a cautionary tale of how even the most well-intentioned game can be corrupted by the dark side of human nature.

**The Great Blizzard Forum Debacle of 2010:** In a bold, and ultimately doomed, attempt to stem the tide of toxicity on its forums, Blizzard Entertainment announced in 2010 that it would be requiring users to post with their real names. The backlash was immediate and ferocious. Players, who had grown accustomed to the anonymity of their online personas, saw this as an invasion of privacy and a betrayal of the very principles of the internet. The outcry was so great that Blizzard was forced to reverse its decision just a few days later. This incident serves as a powerful reminder of the importance of anonymity in online culture, for better or for worse. While anonymity can certainly enable toxic behavior, it is also a cherished value for many online users, who see it as a form of protection and a way to express themselves freely. The challenge for game developers, and for society as a whole, is to find a way to balance the desire for anonymity with the need for a safe and respectful online environment.

### DISCUSSION

The implications of online toxicity extend far beyond the digital realm. The behavior we exhibit in online games is not just a reflection of our “gamer self”; it’s a reflection of our true self, albeit a disinhibited and often uglier version. The way we treat anonymous strangers online says a lot about our capacity for empathy, our ability to handle frustration, and our underlying biases. For consumer behavior researchers, the world of online gaming is a goldmine of data. It’s a living laboratory where you can observe human interaction in its rawest form, unburdened by the social niceties that govern our everyday lives.

The gaming industry, for its part, has a complex and often contradictory relationship with toxicity. On the one hand, game developers have a vested interest in creating a positive and welcoming environment for their players. A toxic community can drive away new players and hurt the long-term health of a game. On the other hand, the very mechanics that make games engaging – competition, challenge, and a sense of mastery – can also be the very things that fuel toxicity. Furthermore, as Constance Steinkuehler’s research suggests, there is a financial disincentive for companies to take a hard line on toxicity [5]. Banning a toxic player might be the right thing to do, but it’s also one less person buying in-game items. This creates a moral hazard, where companies are incentivized to tolerate a certain level of toxicity in the name of profit.

But there is hope. The growing awareness of the problem of online toxicity, both within the gaming community and in the wider culture, is a positive first step. Players are starting to demand more from game developers, and companies are beginning to realize that a healthy community is a valuable asset. The rise of community-led initiatives, such as the “GG” (Good Game) movement, and the increasing popularity of cooperative, non-competitive games, are all signs that the tide may be turning. Ultimately, the solution to online toxicity will require a multi-faceted approach, one that involves better game design, more effective moderation tools, and a cultural shift in how we think about and interact with each other online.

### CONCLUSION

So, why are people meaner behind screens? The answer, as we’ve seen, is a complex cocktail of anonymity, deindividuation, and the high-stakes pressure of competitive gaming. The digital world, with its lack of social cues and consequences, can bring out the worst in us, transforming us from mild-mannered citizens into keyboard-warriors of the highest order. But it doesn’t have to be this way. By understanding the psychological forces at play, we can begin to design better online spaces, and, more importantly, we can begin to be better digital citizens.

The next time you find yourself on the verge of unleashing a torrent of abuse on a teammate who just cost you the game, take a deep breath. Remember that behind that avatar is a real person, a person who is probably just as frustrated as you are. And remember that the goal of gaming, at the end of the day, is to have fun. So, be kind, be patient, and for the love of all that is holy, just say “gg.”
references:
[1] Festinger, L., Pepitone, A., & Newcomb, T. (1952). Some consequences of de-individuation in a group. Journal of Abnormal and Social Psychology, 47(2, Suppl), 382–389. https://doi.org/10.1037/h0057906

[2] The Psychology of Games. (2010, February 18). Anonymity and Antisocial Behavior. https://www.psychologyofgames.com/2010/02/anonymity-and-antisocial-behavior/

[3] Tang, W. Y., & Fox, J. (2016). Men’s harassment behavior in online video games: Personality traits and game factors. Aggressive Behavior, 42(6), 531–540. https://doi.org/10.1002/ab.21646

[4] Kou, Y., & Nardi, B. (2014). Taming the Trolls: How Players Socialize and Moderate in League of Legends. Proceedings of the 2014 CHI Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems, 1999–2008. https://doi.org/10.1145/2556288.2557083

[5] University of California. (2025, December 4). From toxicity to healing: Changing the culture of online gaming. https://www.universityofcalifornia.edu/news/toxicity-healing-changing-culture-online-gaming

[6] Beres, N. A., Frommel, J., Reid, E., & Mandryk, R. L. (2021). Don't you know that you're toxic: Normalization of toxicity in online gaming. Proceedings of the 2021 CHI Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems, 1–15. https://doi.org/10.1145/3411764.3445157
